name: Issun-boshi
description: Issun-boshi, also known as 'One-Inch Boy,' is a prominent figure in Japanese folklore, embodying themes of courage, resourcefulness, and the overcoming of physical limitations. He is often depicted as a miniature hero, no larger than a finger, who embarks on a journey to prove his worth and find his place in the world. His story, deeply rooted in oral tradition and later formalized in Otogizōshi (narrative picture scrolls), serves as an allegory for the potential that resides within even the smallest and seemingly insignificant individuals. Issun-boshi's adventures often involve encounters with demons, princesses, and the acquisition of magical objects, highlighting his ability to outwit larger adversaries through cleverness and bravery. He is a symbol of aspiration and the triumph of spirit over physical disadvantage, resonating with audiences across generations.
subtitle: One-Inch Boy

keyMyths.title: The Birth and Journey of Issun-boshi
keyMyths.description: An elderly, childless couple prayed for a son, even a son as small as their finger. Their prayers were answered, and Issun-boshi was born. As he grew, he remained incredibly small. Determined to make something of himself, he set off on a journey to the capital, armed with a needle sword, a bowl boat, and chopstick paddles. This journey symbolizes his departure from the familiar and his embrace of challenges.
keyMyths.source: Ozaki, Yei Theodora. *The Japanese Fairy Book*.

keyMyths.title: Issun-boshi and the Princess
keyMyths.description: In the capital, Issun-boshi gained employment as a retainer to a wealthy lord and protected his daughter, a beautiful princess. One day, while traveling, they were attacked by a group of Oni (demons). Despite his size, Issun-boshi bravely fought the demons, using his needle sword to sting their eyes and bodies. He and the princess demonstrated courage and quick-thinking while facing danger.
keyMyths.source: Hearn, Lafcadio. *Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things*.

keyMyths.title: The Magic Mallet and Transformation
keyMyths.description: After defeating the demons, they left behind a magical Uchide no Kozuchi (mallet). The princess, recognizing its power, wished for Issun-boshi to grow to a normal size. The mallet granted her wish, and Issun-boshi transformed into a full-sized samurai. He then married the princess and lived happily ever after, a testament to his bravery and the rewards of perseverance. This final act symbolizes the fulfillment of his potential.
